solution.p: We will factor 3r^{2}-11r+6 where a=3, b=-11, and c=6.
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (3) \cdot (6) = 18.

The number 18 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(18)\amp -1(-18)\amp 2(9)\amp -2(-9)
ewline3(6)\amp -3(-6)\amp \end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of -11, and this sum happens with (-9)+(-2)=-11 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]3r^{2}-11r+6\amp =3r^{2}-9r-2r+6
ewline \amp =3r\mathopen{}\left(r-3ight)-2\mathopen{}\left(r-3ight)
ewline \amp =\left(3r-2ight)\mathopen{}\left(r-3ight)\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in 3r^{2}-11r+6 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 3, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(3)\amp \end{array}
The third term, 6, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(6)\amp -1(-6)\amp 2(3)\amp -2(-3)\newline\end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with 3r^{2}-11r+6 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:
Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]3r^{2}-11r+6\amp =\left(3r-2\right)\mathopen{}\left(r-3\right)\end{aligned}
